fragments.content: QT prolongation is a delay in ventricular repolarization measured on ECG as prolonged QT interval (corrected for heart rate as QTc). Normal QTc is <450ms in men and <470ms in women. Prolongation >500ms or increase >60ms from baseline significantly increases risk of torsades de pointes (TdP), a polymorphic ventricular tachycardia that can degenerate into ventricular fibrillation and sudden death. Drugs cause QT prolongation by blocking the IKr potassium channel (hERG channel). High-risk classes include: Class IA and III antiarrhythmics, antipsychotics (haloperidol, thioridazine, ziprasidone), antidepressants (TCAs, citalopram), antibiotics (macrolides, fluoroquinolones), antiemetics (ondansetron, droperidol), methadone, and some antifungals. CredibleMeds.org maintains updated lists of QT-prolonging drugs by risk category.

Management of drug-induced QT prolongation: (1) Discontinue offending agent(s); (2) Correct electrolyte abnormalities (maintain K+ >4.0 mEq/L, Mg2+ >2.0 mg/dL); (3) Avoid concomitant QT-prolonging drugs and CYP inhibitors that increase drug levels; (4) For TdP with hemodynamic instability, immediate defibrillation; (5) For stable TdP, IV magnesium sulfate 2g bolus followed by infusion; (6) Consider temporary overdrive pacing or isoproterenol to increase heart rate; (7) Monitor continuously until QTc normalizes. Prevention includes baseline ECG before starting high-risk drugs, follow-up ECG at steady state, avoiding multiple QT-prolonging agents, dose adjustment in renal/hepatic impairment, and monitoring drug levels when available. Patients with congenital long QT syndrome require special consideration.

Risk stratification for drug-induced TdP: High risk factors include QTc >500ms, history of TdP, congenital long QT syndrome, bradycardia, heart failure, female sex, age >65, electrolyte disturbances (hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, hypocalcemia), renal/hepatic impairment, and drug interactions increasing QT-prolonging drug levels. The Tisdale Risk Score predicts QT prolongation in hospitalized patients (points for age, sex, diuretics, QTc duration, acute MI, sepsis, number of QT-prolonging drugs). FDA requires thorough QT studies for new drugs to assess proarrhythmic potential. Drugs are categorized as: Known risk (clear evidence of TdP), Possible risk (some evidence but insufficient), Conditional risk (risk under specific conditions), or No known risk. Serial ECG monitoring is recommended when initiating or escalating high-risk medications.
